english: Neues Museum Berlin. Yilmaz and Verumius walk through the galleries together. Art provokes questions about identity, origin, and belonging.
Learn core vocabulary for identity and culture
Discuss what belonging means
Begin thinking about diversity and tolerance

Neues Museum Berlin. Hushed halls. Golden light filters through high arched windows. Yilmaz and Verumius walk slowly past ancient artifacts. They are no longer teacher and student. They are two people thinking together.
Look at this collection. Objects from a thousand cultures, under one roof. Whoever knows their heritage understands the world better.
But what is identity? I come from everywhere and nowhere. Is that a strength or a weakness?
Both. I am Turkish and German. This part of me and that one contradict each other sometimes. But diversity is not a weakness.
identity
From Latin 'identitas' (sameness). 'Wer bin ich?' is the\nfundamental Identitätsfrage in German discourse.

A: What does identity mean to you?
B: Identity is who I really am.
A: Can identity change?
B: Yes, it develops throughout a lifetime.

From Latin 'identitas' (sameness). The Fugen-s creates compounds: Identitätskrise (identity crisis), Identitätspolitik (identity politics). In German, Identität covers personal, cultural, and national dimensions.
consciousness, awareness
'Bewusst' (aware) + 'sein' (being). Literally: the state\nof being aware. Philosophers love this word.

A: What is cultural awareness?
B: The ability to understand other cultures.
A: How does one develop awareness?
B: Through traveling, reading, and listening.

'Bewusst' (aware) + 'sein' (being). Literally: the state of being aware. Philosophers from Hegel to Habermas have written volumes about Bewusstsein. 'Kulturelles Bewusstsein' (cultural awareness) is a modern educational goal.
origin, heritage
'Her' (from/hither) + 'Kunft' (coming). Where you come\nfrom. 'Herkunftsland' appears on every German form.

A: Where is your heritage from?
B: My family comes from Turkey.
A: Heritage shapes us all.
B: Yes, but it does not define us.
'Her' (from/hither) + 'Kunft' (coming). 'Herkunftsland' (country of origin) appears on every German form. Your Herkunft shapes but need not define you.
tradition
From Latin 'traditio' (handing over). Same word internationally.\nGermans distinguish Brauch (living custom) from Tradition.

A: Which traditions do you maintain?
B: Christmas and Sugar Feast.
A: Two traditions in one family?
B: Yes, that is normal for us.
From Latin 'traditio' (handing over). Germans distinguish between living traditions (Bräuche) and inherited customs (Überlieferungen). 'Traditionen pflegen' means to maintain traditions actively.
diversity, variety
'Viel' (many) + 'Falt' (fold). Literally: many-foldedness.\n'Kulturelle Vielfalt' is celebrated in modern German politics.

A: What makes Berlin special?
B: The cultural diversity.
A: Diversity means richness?
B: Yes, diversity enriches everyone.
'Viel' (many) + 'Falt' (fold). Literally: many-foldedness. 'Kulturelle Vielfalt' (cultural diversity) is celebrated in modern German political discourse. The word implies richness in difference.
tolerance
From Latin 'tolerantia' (endurance). Lessing's play 'Nathan\nder Weise' (1779) made Toleranz a central German value.

A: What does tolerance mean?
B: Accepting others as they are.
A: Is tolerance always easy?
B: No, but it is necessary.
From Latin 'tolerantia' (endurance). Lessing's play 'Nathan der Weise' (1779) made Toleranz a central German value. The Ring Parable argues all religions deserve equal respect.
prejudice
'Vor' (before) + 'Urteil' (judgment). A judgment made\nbefore knowing the facts. The word reveals the problem.

A: Do you have prejudices?
B: Everyone does, unfortunately.
A: Can one reduce prejudices?
B: Yes, through encounter and dialogue.
'Vor' (before) + 'Urteil' (judgment). A judgment made before knowing the facts. 'Vorurteile abbauen' (to reduce prejudices). The word itself reveals the problem: judging before examining.
perception
'Wahr' (true) + 'Nehmung' (taking). Literally: taking in\ntruth. How you perceive the world around you.

A: Perception is subjective.
B: Yes, everyone sees the world differently.
A: Can one train perception?
B: Yes, through conscious observation.
'Wahr' (true) + 'Nehmung' (taking). Literally: taking in truth. 'Die Wahrnehmung verändert sich' (perception changes). A philosophical powerhouse of a word.
belonging, affiliation
'Zu' (to) + 'gehören' (to belong) + '-igkeit.' The feeling\nof being part of something. What every immigrant seeks.

A: Do you feel a sense of belonging to Berlin?
B: Yes, by now I do.
A: Belonging takes time.
B: And openness from both sides.
'Zu' (to) + 'gehören' (to belong) + '-igkeit.' The feeling of being part of something. 'Ein Gefühl der Zugehörigkeit' (a sense of belonging). What every immigrant seeks.
coexistence, living together
'Zusammen' (together) + 'Leben' (life/living). 'Friedliches\nZusammenleben' is peaceful coexistence. An ongoing project.

A: How does coexistence work?
B: Through respect and communication.
A: Peaceful coexistence is the goal?
B: Yes, for everyone in society.
'Zusammen' (together) + 'Leben' (life/living). 'Friedliches Zusammenleben' (peaceful coexistence). Germany's multicultural Zusammenleben is an ongoing project that shapes daily life.
social, societal
'Gesellschaft' (society) + '-lich.' More formal than 'sozial.'\nGesellschaftlich addresses the structure of society.

A: What are societal changes?
B: Changes in the whole of society.
A: Gesellschaftlich or sozial?
B: Gesellschaftlich is more formal and broader.

'Gesellschaft' (society) + '-lich.' More formal than 'sozial.' Gesellschaftlich addresses the structure; sozial addresses the human aspect. 'Gesellschaftliche Veränderungen' (societal changes) shape the big picture.
multicultural
'Multi' (many) + 'kulturell' (cultural). Germany debated\nfor decades whether it was multikulturell. The answer: yes.

A: Is Berlin multicultural?
B: Absolutely. People from all over the world.
A: A multicultural city thrives on diversity.
B: That is what makes Berlin so special.
'Multi' (many) + 'kulturell' (cultural). Germany debated for decades whether it was multikulturell. The answer: yes, whether anyone planned it or not. Berlin is the prime example.
intercultural
'Inter' (between) + 'kulturell.' Between cultures. Multi-\nmeans many cultures existing. Inter- means cultures interacting.

A: What is intercultural competence?
B: The ability to mediate between cultures.
A: Is that a qualification?
B: Yes, in many German professions.

'Inter' (between) + 'kulturell.' 'Interkulturelle Kompetenz' (intercultural competence) is a formal job qualification in Germany. Multi- means many cultures existing. Inter- means cultures interacting.
tolerant
From Latin 'tolerans.' Same word internationally.\n'Tolerant sein' is easier said than practiced.

A: Are you tolerant?
B: I try every day.
A: A tolerant society requires work.
B: Yes, tolerance does not come by itself.
From Latin 'tolerans.' Same word internationally. The adjective form of Toleranz. 'Eine tolerante Gesellschaft' (a tolerant society). 'Tolerant sein' is easier said than practiced.
traditional
'Tradition' + '-ell.' The -ell ending comes from French\nand marks formal adjectives. Can be positive or conservative.

A: Are those traditional values?
B: Yes, for generations.
A: Traditional can be positive.
B: Or conservative. Depends on the context.
'Tradition' + '-ell.' The -ell ending comes from French and marks formal adjectives. Traditionell can be positive (heritage) or conservative (resistance to change).
to reflect
From Latin 'reflectere' (to bend back). Both literal (light)\nand figurative (thought). Non-separable -ieren verb.

A: What are you reflecting on?
B: On my identity.
A: It is important to reflect.
B: Yes, especially in a new culture.
From Latin 'reflectere' (to bend back). Both literal (light) and figurative (thought). Non-separable -ieren verb. 'Über seine Identität reflektieren' (to reflect on one's identity).
to perceive, to notice
'Wahr' (true) + 'nehmen' (to take). Separable: 'Ich nehme\nwahr.' Taking in what is true. Active observation.

A: Do you perceive the differences?
B: Yes, every day.
A: Consciously perceiving your surroundings is important.
B: It changes the perspective.
'Wahr' (true) + 'nehmen' (to take). Separable: 'Ich nehme wahr.' Taking in what is true. Active observation. The noun Wahrnehmung is one of Germany's great philosophical words.
to question critically, to scrutinize
'Hinter' (behind) + 'fragen' (to ask). Not separable despite\nhinter-. You ask behind the surface. Going deeper.

A: Should one question traditions critically?
B: Yes, always.
A: Questioning critically does not mean rejecting.
B: Exactly. It means wanting to understand.
'Hinter' (behind) + 'fragen' (to ask). Not separable despite hinter-. You ask behind the surface. Going deeper than a simple Frage. 'Traditionen hinterfragen' (to question traditions critically).
Where you come from. 'Her' (from) + 'Kunft' (coming). Your origin and your roots.
From 'zugehören' (to belong). The feeling of being part of something.
The Latin-derived word for acceptance of differences. Lessing wrote about it.
In a multicultural society, tolerance is especially important.
Build a vocabulary of values, beliefs, and worldviews. Understand the philosophical concepts that Germans debate.
Learn core vocabulary for values and beliefs
Understand philosophical concepts in German
Articulate your own values
Room 2.10, North Dome Hall. Nefertiti behind glass, three thousand years old. Yilmaz and Verumius stand before her, audio guides forgotten.
She was found in Amarna, exhibited in Berlin, reclaimed by Egypt. Who does culture belong to? Who gets to define what identity means?
view, perspective, worldview
'An' (at) + 'Schauung' (viewing). 'Weltanschauung' (worldview)\nis one of Germany's most famous word exports.

A: What is your view?
B: I believe in tolerance and openness.
A: A positive worldview.
B: Yes, shaped by experience.
'An' (at) + 'Schauung' (viewing). 'Weltanschauung' (worldview) is one of Germany's most famous exports. Kant used Anschauung as a philosophical term for intuitive perception.
conviction, firm belief
'Über' (over) + 'Zeugung' (producing). Literally: producing\nsomething overwhelming. Stronger than Meinung (opinion).

A: Do you act from conviction?
B: Yes, always.
A: Conviction gives strength.
B: Without conviction one is disoriented.
'Über' (over) + 'Zeugung' (producing). Literally: producing something overwhelming. 'Aus Überzeugung handeln' (to act from conviction). Stronger than Meinung (opinion).
concept, idea, imagination
'Vor' (before) + 'Stellung' (position). At B2 level, the\nabstract meaning dominates: your idea of something.

A: What is your idea of happiness?
B: Time with family.
A: Everyone has different ideas.
B: That makes life interesting.
'Vor' (before) + 'Stellung' (position). Already known as 'introduction/interview' from B1. At B2: the abstract meaning dominates. 'Meine Vorstellung von Glück' (my idea of happiness).
value system, moral conception
'Wert' (value) + 'Vorstellung' (concept). No Fugenelement\nbetween the parts. Your ideas about what matters most.

A: What values do you hold?
B: Honesty and respect.
A: Values shape our actions.
B: Yes, they are our inner compass.
'Wert' (value) + 'Vorstellung' (concept). The Fugen-absence here is noteworthy: Wert + Vorstellung without a connecting element. Your ideas about what matters.
self-image
'Selbst' (self) + 'Bild' (image). Contrast: Fremdbild (how\nothers see you). The gap between the two is where crises live.

A: Does your self-image match reality?
B: Not always.
A: Self-image changes.
B: Yes, with every new experience.
'Selbst' (self) + 'Bild' (image). 'Sein Selbstbild verändern' (to change one's self-image). Contrast: Fremdbild (how others see you). The gap between the two is where identity crises live.
concept, plan
From Latin 'conceptum' (something conceived). In German,\nKonzept also means 'rough draft.' Both senses: something formed.

A: Do you have a concept?
B: Yes, for the project.
A: A good concept takes time.
B: But it saves work later.
From Latin 'conceptum' (something conceived). In German, Konzept also means 'rough draft.' 'Ein Konzept entwickeln' (to develop a concept). Both senses share the idea of something being formed.
philosophy
From Greek 'philosophia' (love of wisdom). Germany produced\nKant, Hegel, Marx, Nietzsche, Heidegger, Arendt.

A: Are you interested in philosophy?
B: Yes, especially German philosophy.
A: Which philosopher inspires you?
B: Hannah Arendt. She thought radically.
From Greek 'philosophia' (love of wisdom). Germany produced Kant, Hegel, Marx, Nietzsche, Heidegger, Arendt. 'Deutsche Philosophie' is a major academic tradition.
ethics
From Greek 'ethike' (moral philosophy). 'Ethik-Kommission'\nreviews medical research and AI projects in Germany.

A: What is ethics?
B: The study of right action.
A: Do we need ethics in technology?
B: Absolutely. Without ethics, progress is dangerous.
From Greek 'ethike' (moral philosophy). 'Ethik-Kommission' (ethics committee) reviews medical research and AI projects in Germany. 'Berufsethik' (professional ethics) is taken seriously.
fundamental value
'Grund' (foundation) + 'Wert' (value). The Grundwerte of\nthe Grundgesetz: human dignity, freedom, equality.

A: What are Germany's fundamental values?
B: Human dignity, freedom, equality.
A: Fundamental values are non-negotiable.
B: Exactly, they are the basis of everything.
'Grund' (foundation) + 'Wert' (value). 'Die Grundwerte des Grundgesetzes' (the fundamental values of the constitution): human dignity, freedom, equality. Foundation values that cannot be changed.
quality of life
'Leben' (life) + 's' (Fugen-s) + 'Qualität' (quality).\nGerman cities regularly rank among the highest worldwide.

A: How is the quality of life in Berlin?
B: High, despite the hustle.
A: What makes good quality of life?
B: Green spaces, culture, and affordable rent.
'Leben' (life) + 's' (Fugen-s) + 'Qualität' (quality). The Fugen-s connects the two parts. German cities regularly rank among the highest for Lebensqualität worldwide.
human dignity
'Menschen' (human) + 'Würde' (dignity). Article 1 of the\nGerman constitution: 'Die Würde des Menschen ist unantastbar.'

A: What is human dignity?
B: The right to be respected as a human being.
A: Is human dignity in the constitution?
B: Yes, Article 1. The most important sentence.
'Menschen' (human) + 'Würde' (dignity). Article 1 of the German constitution: 'Die Würde des Menschen ist unantastbar' (Human dignity is inviolable). The most important sentence in German law.
independent, autonomous
'Eigen' (own) + 'ständig' (standing). You stand on your own.\nNot relying on others for your opinions or identity.

A: Do you think independently?
B: I always try to.
A: Independent thinking is important.
B: Especially in a democracy.
'Eigen' (own) + 'ständig' (standing). 'Eigenständig denken' (to think independently). You stand on your own. Not relying on others for your opinions or identity.
open
Same Germanic root as English 'open.' Karl Popper's\nconcept of the 'open society' deeply influenced Germany.

A: Are you open to new things?
B: Yes, always.
A: An open society needs open people.
B: I fully agree.
Same Germanic root as English 'open.' 'Offen für Neues sein' (to be open to new things). Karl Popper's concept of the 'open society' deeply influenced German postwar thought.
respectful
'Respekt' (respect) + 'voll' (full). Full of respect. The\n-voll suffix works like English '-ful': liebevoll, hoffnungsvoll.

A: Was the conversation respectful?
B: Yes, despite different opinions.
A: Respectful interaction is the basis.
B: Without respect, no dialogue.
'Respekt' (respect) + 'voll' (full). Full of respect. The -voll suffix works like English '-ful': liebevoll (loving), hoffnungsvoll (hopeful), respektvoll (respectful).
cosmopolitan, open to the world
'Welt' (world) + 'offen' (open). Berlin calls itself weltoffen.\nThe word captures Germany's self-image as globally minded.

A: Is Hamburg cosmopolitan?
B: Yes, as a port city, always.
A: Cosmopolitanism is a strength.
B: It comes from international trade.
'Welt' (world) + 'offen' (open). 'Eine weltoffene Stadt' (a cosmopolitan city). Berlin calls itself weltoffen. The word captures Germany's self-image as globally minded.
diverse, manifold
'Viel' (many) + 'fältig' (fold). Many-folded, multi-layered,\nrich in variety. The adjective form of Vielfalt.

A: The program is diverse.
B: Yes, something for everyone.
A: Diverse offerings enrich everyone.
B: Diversity is always good.
'Viel' (many) + 'fältig' (fold). Related to Vielfalt. The adjective form. Many-folded, multi-layered, rich in variety. 'Eine vielfältige Kultur' (a diverse culture).
to respect
From Latin 'respectare' (to look back at). Non-separable\n-ieren verb. Same word across European languages.

A: Do you respect other cultures?
B: Of course.
A: Respecting means taking seriously.
B: Yes, not just tolerating, but valuing.
From Latin 'respectare' (to look back at). 'Andere Kulturen respektieren' (to respect other cultures). Non-separable -ieren verb. Same word across European languages.
to shape, to coin, to imprint
'Die Kindheit prägt den Menschen' (Childhood shapes a person).\nAlso: 'eine Münze prägen' (to mint a coin). Deeply formative.

A: What shapes a person?
B: Family, culture, and experiences.
A: Berlin has shaped me.
B: Every city shapes its residents.
Something stamped into you that remains. 'Die Kindheit prägt den Menschen' (Childhood shapes a person). Also: 'eine Münze prägen' (to mint a coin). Deeply formative.
to care for, to maintain, to cultivate
'Traditionen pflegen' (to cultivate traditions). Also:\n'Kranke pflegen' (to care for the sick). Nurturing in all forms.

A: Do you maintain your traditions?
B: Yes, that is important to me.
A: To maintain means to actively preserve.
B: Exactly, not just passively remember.
'Traditionen pflegen' (to cultivate traditions). Also: 'Kranke pflegen' (to care for the sick). 'Pflegeversicherung' (nursing care insurance) is one of Germany's five social insurances.
to acknowledge, to recognize officially
'An' (at) + 'erkennen' (to recognize). Not separable despite\nthe an-. The official stamp of acceptance.

A: Is your degree recognized?
B: Yes, after a long review.
A: Recognition is important for one's career.
B: Without recognition, little is possible.
'An' (at) + 'erkennen' (to recognize). 'Einen Abschluss anerkennen' (to recognize a degree). Not separable despite the an-. The official stamp of acceptance.
A compound noun enshrined in Article 1 of the German constitution. Identify the two root elements: the being and the quality being protected.
To form and shape. Childhood stamps something into a person that stays.
Childhood shapes a person for their whole life.
The small -s- that connects two parts of a compound noun. A phonetic bridge.
Think independently. Question values. Stay open. Easier said than done when you do not even know where you come from.
Master generalized relative clauses for universal statements. Discuss migration, integration, and identity in German.
Learn generalized relative clauses (wer..., der...; alles, was...)
Discuss migration and integration
Build complex argumentative sentences
Whoever lives between two cultures sees the world differently. Everything I have experienced has shaped me: Istanbul, Berlin, the language in between.
What surprises me the most is the question itself. What does it mean to belong somewhere?
Later. Heinrich's apartment. Warm, cluttered with baking memorabilia. Bread awards on the wall. Heinrich sits in his armchair, a blanket over his knees. Verumius reads Die Zeit aloud.
Not Wirtschaft. WIRT-schaft. The emphasis! Whoever speaks correctly is also understood correctly.
Heinrich hears worse than last year. But his corrections are as precise as ever. Everything this man knows, he carries in his voice.
migration
From Latin 'migratio' (wandering). Germany's 2015\nMigrationsdebatte changed the political landscape.

A: How has migration changed Germany?
B: Fundamentally. 26 percent have a migration background.
A: Migration brings diversity.
B: And challenges. Both.
From Latin 'migratio' (wandering). Germany's 2015 Migrationsdebatte changed the political landscape. 'Migrationshintergrund' (migration background) is a German census category: 21 million people.
integration
From Latin 'integratio' (renewal). 'Integration ist keine\nEinbahnstraße' (not a one-way street) is a common German saying.

A: How does integration succeed?
B: Through language, work, and respect.
A: Integration requires both sides.
B: Yes, it is not a one-way street.
From Latin 'integratio' (renewal/restoration). 'Integration ist keine Einbahnstraße' (Integration is not a one-way street) is a common German saying. Both sides must move.
immigration
'Ein' (in) + 'Wanderung' (wandering). Germany was officially\n'kein Einwanderungsland' until 2005. Reality preceded policy.

A: Is Germany an immigration country?
B: Yes, officially since 2005.
A: Immigration has changed history.
B: Millions of people came and stayed.

'Ein' (in) + 'Wanderung' (wandering). Germany was officially 'kein Einwanderungsland' (not a country of immigration) until 2005 when it passed its first immigration law. Reality preceded policy by decades.
discrimination
From Latin 'discriminare' (to distinguish). The AGG (General\nEqual Treatment Act) of 2006 prohibits it in employment.

A: Have you experienced discrimination?
B: Unfortunately yes.
A: Discrimination is legally prohibited.
B: But it still exists despite that.
From Latin 'discriminare' (to distinguish). The Allgemeines Gleichbehandlungsgesetz (AGG, General Equal Treatment Act) of 2006 prohibits Diskriminierung in employment and daily life.
inclusion
From Latin 'inclusio' (enclosure). Beyond disability:\n'Inklusion means everyone belongs.' Active inclusion.

A: What does inclusion mean?
B: Everyone belongs, without exception.
A: Inclusion goes further than tolerance.
B: Yes, it means actively including.
From Latin 'inclusio' (enclosure). Beyond disability: Inklusion means everyone belongs. Germany's education system is moving toward Inklusion in schools. Not just tolerance, but active inclusion.
social environment, milieu
From French 'milieu' (middle/environment). Sinus-Milieus\ndivide German society into lifestyle groups.

A: What social environment do you come from?
B: From an academic milieu.
A: The milieu shapes the worldview.
B: Yes, it forms how we think.
From French 'milieu' (middle/environment). Sinus-Milieus divide German society into lifestyle groups: liberal-intellectual, traditional, performer, etc. Your Milieu shapes your Weltanschauung.
generational conflict
'Generationen' (generations) + 'Konflikt.' The Fugen-en\nconnects the parts. Every generation has its defining conflict.

A: Is there a generational conflict in your family?
B: Yes, on the topic of climate.
A: Every generation has its conflict.
B: The 68ers against their parents, we against climate change.
'Generationen' (generations) + 'Konflikt.' Every generation in Germany has its defining conflict: 68ers vs. parents, Gen Z vs. Boomers on climate. The Fugen-en connects the parts.
change, transformation
From 'wandeln' (to change/walk). Gradual, deep change: not\na revolution but an evolution. Wandel takes time.

A: Do you see societal change?
B: Yes, society is becoming more open.
A: Change is slow but steady.
B: Better slow than not at all.
From 'wandeln' (to change/walk). Wandel implies gradual, deep change: not a revolution but an evolution. 'Wandel durch Annäherung' (change through rapprochement) was Willy Brandt's Ostpolitik.
way of life, lifestyle
'Lebens' (life's) + 'Weise' (way/manner). The Fugen-s links\nLeben to Weise. How you choose to live your life.

A: Which lifestyle do you prefer?
B: Simple and conscious.
A: Alternative lifestyles are accepted.
B: In Berlin, definitely.
'Lebens' (life's) + 'Weise' (way/manner). The Fugen-s links Leben to Weise. 'Alternative Lebensweisen' (alternative lifestyles) became a political topic in the 1970s.
worldview
'Welt' (world) + 'Anschauung' (view). One of the most\nfamous German words exported to English. Kant used it first.

A: What is your worldview?
B: Humanistic and cosmopolitan.
A: A worldview is more than an opinion.
B: Yes, it is an entire system of thought.
'Welt' (world) + 'Anschauung' (view). One of the most famous German words exported to English. Kant used it first. Your comprehensive framework for understanding everything.
ethnic
From Greek 'ethnikos' (of a people). Used carefully in German\ndiscourse due to historical sensitivity.

A: What does ethnic diversity mean?
B: People of different origins together.
A: Ethnic is a sensitive word.
B: Yes, one uses it carefully.
From Greek 'ethnikos' (of a people). 'Ethnische Vielfalt' (ethnic diversity). A term used carefully in German discourse due to historical sensitivity.
pluralistic
'Plural' + '-istisch.' Many worldviews coexisting. The ideal\nof the Grundgesetz: no single ideology dominates.

A: Is Germany pluralistic?
B: Yes, many opinions coexist.
A: A pluralistic society needs dialogue.
B: And the willingness to listen.
'Plural' + '-istisch.' Pluralistic. Many worldviews coexisting. The ideal of the Grundgesetz: no single ideology dominates. 'Eine pluralistische Gesellschaft' (a pluralistic society).
cosmopolitan
From Greek 'kosmopolites' (citizen of the world). Berlin\nconsiders itself kosmopolitisch. Comfort with global diversity.

A: Do you feel cosmopolitan?
B: In Berlin, definitely.
A: Cosmopolitan means living open to the world.
B: Yes, feeling at home in the world.
From Greek 'kosmopolites' (citizen of the world). 'Eine kosmopolitische Einstellung' (a cosmopolitan attitude). Berlin considers itself kosmopolitisch. The word implies comfort with global diversity.
to integrate
From Latin 'integrare' (to make whole). Non-separable -ieren\nverb. Both transitive and reflexive.

A: How can one integrate?
B: Learn the language and participate.
A: Integration begins with wanting to.
B: And with the openness of society.
From Latin 'integrare' (to make whole). 'Sich in eine Gesellschaft integrieren' (to integrate into a society). Non-separable -ieren verb. Both transitive and reflexive.
to engage critically with, to confront
'Auseinander' (apart) + 'setzen' (to set) + 'mit' (with).\nLiterally: to set things apart with something. Intellectual verb.

A: Do you engage critically with prejudices?
B: Yes, actively.
A: To engage critically means to examine.
B: Not simply accept, but question.

'Auseinander' (apart) + 'setzen' (to set) + 'mit' (with). Literally: to set things apart with something. 'Sich mit Vorurteilen auseinandersetzen' (to confront prejudices). The most intellectual reflexive verb in German.
to identify with
'Identifizieren' (to identify) + 'mit' (with). Reflexive.\nFinding yourself in something external.

A: Do you identify with Berlin?
B: Yes, the city has become part of me.
A: To identify with means to feel belonging.
B: Exactly, it is a feeling of connection.
'Identifizieren' (to identify) + 'mit' (with). Reflexive. 'Sich mit einer Kultur identifizieren' (to identify with a culture). Finding yourself in something external.
to adapt, to adjust
'An' (at/to) + 'passen' (to fit). Separable and reflexive.\nFitting yourself to new circumstances without losing yourself.

A: Do you adapt quickly?
B: Yes, I had to learn that.
A: Adapting is not giving up who you are.
B: Right. Adapting and preserving at the same time.
'An' (at/to) + 'passen' (to fit). Separable: 'Ich passe mich an.' Fitting yourself to new circumstances. Not losing yourself, but adjusting. A survival skill.
to mediate, to convey
'Ver-' + 'Mittel' (middle/means). Being the middle person.\nAlso: 'Wissen vermitteln' (to convey knowledge). A bridge function.

A: Can you mediate between cultures?
B: I try that every day.
A: To mediate means to build bridges.
B: Yes, between different worlds.
'Ver-' + 'Mittel' (middle/means). 'Zwischen Kulturen vermitteln' (to mediate between cultures). Being the middle person. Also: 'Wissen vermitteln' (to convey knowledge). A bridge function.

German has special relative clauses for universal statements. No specific person, but anyone who fits.

wer..., der...: 'Wer tolerant ist, der gewinnt Freunde.'
(Whoever is tolerant gains friends.)

was..., das...: 'Was er sagt, das stimmt.'
(What he says is true.)

alles, was...: 'Alles, was ich erlebt habe, hat mich geformt.'
(Everything I experienced shaped me.)

nichts, was...: 'Nichts, was er sagt, überrascht mich.'
(Nothing he says surprises me.)

das, was...: 'Das, was zählt, ist Respekt.'
(That which counts is respect.)

The wer-clause is a free relative clause: no antecedent noun needed. The verb goes to the end of the relative clause.

Wer, wen, wem function like relative pronouns:

Nominative: Wer tolerant ist, der gewinnt.
Accusative: Wen man respektiert, den hoert man.
Dative: Wem man hilft, dem hilft man gern.

The demonstrative pronoun in the main clause (der/den/dem) can be dropped:
'Wer tolerant ist, gewinnt Freunde.' (also correct)

After alles, nichts, etwas, das, always use 'was' (not 'das'):
'Alles, was zaehlt' (not: 'Alles, das zaehlt')
Wer/wen/wem folgen den Kasusregeln. Nach alles/nichts/etwas/das immer 'was,' nie 'das.'
'Wer' without a reference word means 'whoever.' A universal statement.
A relative pronoun for universal statements, not referring to a specific person. It opens a free relative clause followed by 'der' in the main clause.
Whoever knows their heritage understands themselves better.
Master reference words for precise reference in German. Build text cohesion in written and formal language.
Learn reference words: dieser, jener, solcher, derjenige, derselbe
Distinguish similar items with elegance
Build logical discourse with ersterer/letzterer
Back in the museum. Yilmaz points at two sculptures: one Greek, one Egyptian. Both headless. Both three thousand years old. But not the same.
This one here is Greek. That one there is Egyptian. The one who put them together wanted to show: culture knows no borders.
this one, this
Refers to something near or just mentioned. Declines like\na der-word: dieser/diese/dieses/diese. Most common Verweiswort.

A: Which book do you mean?
B: This one here.
A: This value is important to me.
B: To me too. This value connects us.
Refers to something near or just mentioned. 'Dieser Wert ist wichtig' (This value is important). Declines like a der-word: dieser/diese/dieses/diese. The most common Verweiswort.
that one (distant)
Points to something farther away or previously mentioned.\nDeclines like dieser. More literary than spoken.

A: Do you know that artist?
B: That one from the 19th century?
A: Yes, that one lived here.
B: That era was fascinating.
Points to something farther away or previously mentioned. 'Jener Künstler lebte im 19. Jahrhundert' (That artist lived in the 19th century). Declines like dieser. More literary than spoken.
such a one, such
Describes a kind or type. Declines like dieser. 'Solch ein\nMensch' inverts: solch + ein + noun.

A: Such prejudices are dangerous.
B: Yes, such ways of thinking must change.
A: Such a person inspires me.
B: The world needs such people.
Describes a kind or type. 'Solche Vorurteile sind gefährlich' (Such prejudices are dangerous). Declines like dieser. 'Solch ein Mensch' (such a person) inverts: solch + ein + noun.
the one who (masculine)
'Der' + 'jenige' (the particular one). Compound demonstrative.\nBoth parts decline: desjenigen, demjenigen.

A: The one who comes first wins.
B: Who is the one?
A: The one who works the hardest.
B: Then I need to hurry.
'Der' + 'jenige' (the particular one). 'Derjenige, der zuerst kommt, gewinnt' (The one who comes first wins). Compound demonstrative. Both parts decline: desjenigen, demjenigen.
the one who (feminine)
Feminine form of derjenige. Both parts decline together.\nFormal, written register.

A: The one who reads the most knows the most.
B: Is that Yilmaz?
A: Yes, the one who always has a book with her.
B: True, she reads constantly.
Feminine form of derjenige. 'Diejenige, die am meisten liest, weiß am meisten' (The one (f.) who reads the most knows the most). Both parts decline together.
the one which (neuter)
Neuter form of derjenige. Formal, written. In speech,\nGermans often substitute 'das, was.'

A: That which truly counts is respect.
B: True. I would like to read that book.
A: That child playing over there?
B: Yes, the one with the red hat.
Neuter form of derjenige. 'Dasjenige, was wirklich zählt' (That which truly counts). Formal, written. In speech, Germans often substitute 'das, was.'
the same one (masculine)
'Der' + 'selbe' (same). Both parts decline. Implies true\nidentity: literally the same individual or thing.

A: Is that the same man?
B: Yes, the same one as yesterday.
A: The same mistake happens again and again.
B: We must learn from it.
'Der' + 'selbe' (same). Both parts decline. Derselbe implies true identity: literally the same individual or thing. 'Derselbe Fehler passiert immer wieder' (The same mistake happens again and again).
the same one (feminine)
Feminine form of derselbe. Not 'die gleiche' (the similar one).\nDerselbe = identical. Der gleiche = similar but separate.

A: Every generation faces the same question.
B: Yes, always the same one.
A: Is it the same or just a similar one?
B: Dieselbe: identical. Die gleiche: similar.
Feminine form of derselbe. 'Dieselbe Frage stellt sich jede Generation' (Every generation faces the same question). Not to be confused with 'die gleiche' (the similar one). Derselbe = identical.
the same one (neuter)
Neuter form of derselbe. 'Ist das dasselbe oder nur das\nGleiche?' is a famous German distinction.

A: Is that the same thing?
B: No, just a similar one.
A: I had the same feeling too.
B: We share the same experience.

Neuter form of derselbe. 'Ist das dasselbe oder nur das Gleiche?' (Is it the same or just similar?) A famous German distinction. Dasselbe = one and the same object. Das Gleiche = two objects that look alike.
the former
'Erster' (first) + comparative ending. Used in formal writing\nto refer back to the first of two things mentioned.

A: Tolerance and respect: the former is passive, the latter active.
B: Good comparison.
A: The former accepts, the latter values.
B: Both are important.

'Erster' (first) + comparative ending. 'Toleranz und Respekt: Ersterer ist passiv, letzterer aktiv.' Used in formal writing to refer back to two things. Shows you can track two referents across a sentence.
the latter
'Letzter' (last) + comparative ending. Classic academic\npairing with ersterer. Avoids repeating nouns. Written register.

A: Culture and language: the latter connects people.
B: And the former shapes them.
A: The former... the latter... is elegant.
B: Yes, especially in academic texts.
'Letzter' (last) + comparative ending. 'Ersterer... letzterer...' is a classic academic pairing. It avoids repeating nouns. Written register. Shows sophisticated text cohesion.
such a (one)
'Ein' + 'solcher.' The indefinite article version of solcher.\nEin solcher Mensch = a person of that kind.

A: Such a change takes time.
B: Yes, such a process is slow.
A: Such a person inspires me.
B: Such people are rare.
'Ein' + 'solcher.' The indefinite article version of solcher. 'Ein solcher Wandel braucht Zeit' (Such a change takes time). Ein solcher Mensch = a person of that kind.
equally, in equal measure
'Gleicher' (equal) + 'Maßen' (measure). An elegant adverb\nfor balanced comparisons in formal writing.

A: Everyone benefits equally.
B: That is the goal.
A: Equally important are tolerance and respect.
B: Yes, both equally.
'Gleicher' (equal) + 'Maßen' (measure). 'Alle profitieren gleichermaßen' (Everyone benefits equally). An elegant adverb for balanced comparisons.
essentially, in essence
'Im' + 'Wesentlichen' (essential). A qualifier that focuses\non the core. Allows for minor disagreements.

A: Do you agree?
B: Essentially yes.
A: Essentially we are of one opinion.
B: Only in details are there differences.
'Im' + 'Wesentlichen' (essential). 'Im Wesentlichen stimme ich zu' (I essentially agree). A qualifier that focuses on the core. Allows for minor disagreements.
fundamentally, basically
'Im' + 'Grunde' (ground/foundation). Looks at the foundation\nbeneath the surface. What lies at the very bottom.

A: Fundamentally, all people are equal.
B: Yes, fundamentally.
A: Fundamentally we all want the same thing.
B: Belonging and respect.
'Im' + 'Grunde' (ground/foundation). 'Im Grunde sind alle Menschen gleich' (Fundamentally, all people are equal). Looks at the foundation beneath the surface.
by no means, not at all
'Keines' (none) + 'Wegs' (way). Stronger than 'nicht.'\nA formal negation used in academic and political discourse.

A: Is that acceptable?
B: By no means.
A: Keineswegs means: absolutely not.
B: A strong negation.
'Keines' (none) + 'Wegs' (way). 'Das ist keineswegs akzeptabel' (That is by no means acceptable). Stronger than 'nicht.' A formal negation.
under no circumstances
'Keines' (none) + 'Falls' (case). Even stronger than keineswegs.\nAn absolute rejection. Legal and formal register.

A: May that happen?
B: Under no circumstances.
A: Keinesfalls means: never.
B: Even stronger than keineswegs.
'Keines' (none) + 'Falls' (case). 'Das darf keinesfalls passieren' (That must under no circumstances happen). Even stronger than keineswegs. An absolute rejection.
ultimately, in the end
'Letzt' (last) + '-lich.' The concluding word in an argument.\nWhat remains when everything else is stripped away.

A: What ultimately counts?
B: Ultimately, only respect counts.
A: Ultimately we are all equal.
B: Yes, that is the ultimate truth.
'Letzt' (last) + '-lich.' The concluding word in an argument. 'Letztlich zählt nur der Respekt' (Ultimately, only respect counts). What remains when everything else is stripped away.

German has a rich system of reference words (Verweiswörter) that create text cohesion.

dieser/jener: this vs. that (near vs. far)
solcher/ein solcher: such (a type of)
derjenige: the particular one (formal, with relative clause)
derselbe: the identical one (not just similar)
ersterer/letzterer: the former/the latter (academic pair)

All decline like der-words. In writing, they replace repeated nouns and create elegant reference chains.
'Die Wirtschaft und die Kultur: Erstere wächst, letztere blüht.'

With derjenige and derselbe, BOTH parts decline:

Nominative: derjenige / diejenige / dasjenige
Accusative: denjenigen / diejenige / dasjenige
Dative: demjenigen / derjenigen / demjenigen
Genitive: desjenigen / derjenigen / desjenigen

Same with derselbe:
Nominative: derselbe / dieselbe / dasselbe
Accusative: denselben / dieselbe / dasselbe
Dative: demselben / derselben / demselben

The first part (der/die/das/den/dem/des) follows the article.
The second part (-jenige/-selbe) follows adjective declension.
Dieser/jener/solcher: simple declension. Derjenige/derselbe: both parts decline.
The first of a pair refers to whichever noun was named first in the previous sentence. The second refers to the second. Order matches order.
A formal demonstrative that singles out a specific person and leads directly into a relative clause. More precise than 'dieser' or 'jener'.
The one who denies their heritage loses a part of themselves.
'Wer...' and 'alles, was...' are free relative patterns for universal statements.
Formal demonstrative pronoun introducing a relative clause, plus an accusative plural form referring back to what was just mentioned.
The one who has prejudices should confront them.
Dinner table debate: equality, solidarity, and freedom of speech. Along the way, learn the Fugenelement system for compound nouns.
Learn the Fugenelement system for compound nouns
Debate integration, equality, and tolerance
Practice all three grammar constructs together
Mia's apartment, evening. Three place settings, candles, wine. The conversation has moved past small talk into deep territory.
Equal rights are not a gift. Those who demand them must fight for them. In the GDR we had equal opportunity, at least on paper.
Whoever lives between two cultures experiences discrimination and solidarity in equal measure. This contradiction shapes me.
Everything I have learned here shows me: freedom of opinion means that one may also say uncomfortable truths.
We do not agree. Good. Those who always share the same opinion learn nothing from each other.
The next day. Neighborhood cafe. KD in casual clothes, frustrated. His citizens' office is going digital. He holds his phone like a grenade.
Digitalization. Everything is supposed to be digital now. But do you know what happens when the server crashes? Nothing. Paper doesn't crash.
KD types on his phone like on a typewriter. Individual letters. With his index finger. Mia rolls her eyes but smiles.
multiculturalism
Multi + Kulturalitaet. Das abstrakte Konzept.\nKein Fugenelement zwischen multi und Kulturalitaet.

A: Multiculturalism is a reality.
B: Yes, especially in big cities.
A: Is multiculturalism always positive?
B: It brings opportunities and challenges.
Multi + Kulturalitaet. The abstract concept. No Fugenelement between multi and Kulturalitaet. A political term debated intensely since the 1990s in Germany.
equal rights
Gleich (equal) + Berechtigung (authorization).\nArtikel 3 des Grundgesetzes garantiert sie.

A: Equal rights are in the constitution.
B: Since 1949.
A: Is it a reality today?
B: On paper yes, in daily life not always.
Gleich (equal) + Berechtigung (authorization). Article 3 of the Grundgesetz guarantees it. 'Gleichberechtigung von Mann und Frau' was added in 1949 against resistance.
equal opportunity
Chancen + Gleichheit. Das Fugen-n verbindet.\nDas Ideal: alle starten von derselben Position.

A: Is there equal opportunity in Germany?
B: Not for everyone.
A: What is missing?
B: Better education for all social classes.
Chancen (chances) + Gleichheit (equality). The Fugen-n connects the two parts. The ideal that everyone starts from the same position. Germany's three-track school system challenges this ideal.
emancipation
Vom Lateinischen 'emancipatio' (Befreiung).\nUrspruenglich roemisch-rechtlich: ein Sohn wird frei.

A: Emancipation means liberation.
B: From what constraint?
A: From any external determination.
B: Women's emancipation changed Germany.
From Latin 'emancipatio' (freeing from hand). Originally a Roman legal term for a son freed from paternal authority. 'Frauenemanzipation' shaped German politics since 1968.
solidarity
Vom Franzoesischen 'solidarite.' 'Solidaritaetszuschlag'\nwar Deutschlands Steuer für die Wiedervereinigung.

A: Solidarity is important.
B: Especially in times of crisis.
A: What is the solidarity surcharge?
B: A tax for reunification.
From French 'solidarite.' The 'Solidaritaetszuschlag' (solidarity surcharge) was Germany's tax for reunification. East and West Germans paying for each other: solidarity in action.
civil society
Zivil + Gesellschaft. Alles zwischen Staat und Individuum.\nDeutschlands 600.000 Vereine sind das Rueckgrat.

A: What is civil society?
B: Clubs, initiatives, organizations.
A: Why is it important?
B: It fills the gap between state and citizen.
Zivil (civil) + Gesellschaft (society). Organizations, clubs, initiatives: everything between state and individual. Germany's 600,000 Vereine are the backbone of the Zivilgesellschaft.
exclusion, marginalization
Aus (out) + Grenzung (bordering). Eine Grenze ziehen\nund jemanden draussen lassen. Gegenteil von Inklusion.

A: Exclusion is a societal problem.
B: Yes, it affects many groups.
A: How can you fight exclusion?
B: Through inclusion and open dialogue.
Aus (out) + Grenzung (bordering). Drawing a border around someone and pushing them out. 'Soziale Ausgrenzung' (social exclusion) is the opposite of Inklusion.
xenophobia
Fremden (foreigners) + Feindlichkeit (hostility).\nEin Kompositum, das das Problem präzise benennt.

A: Xenophobia unfortunately still exists.
B: In many countries.
A: How do you fight it?
B: Through education and encounter.
Fremden (foreigners) + Feindlichkeit (hostility). A compound that names the problem precisely. Germans discuss Fremdenfeindlichkeit openly as a societal challenge.
acceptance
Vom Lateinischen 'acceptare.' Stärker als Toleranz:\ntolerate = ertragen, accept = annehmen.

A: Acceptance is more than tolerance.
B: What is the difference?
A: To tolerate means to endure. To accept means to embrace.
B: Societal acceptance is the goal.
From Latin 'acceptare.' Stronger than Toleranz: tolerating means enduring, accepting means embracing. 'Gesellschaftliche Akzeptanz' is the next step after tolerance.
Action verbs for social change: discriminate, exclude, emancipate, overcome. Plus adjectives for describing an enlightened, equal society.
Learn verbs of social action
Use adjectives for social values
Discuss equality and inclusion
enlightened
Auf (up) + geklaert (cleared). Von der Aufklaerung.\nKants Definition: der Mut, den eigenen Verstand zu gebrauchen.

A: An enlightened society thinks critically.
B: Yes, that is the legacy of the Enlightenment.
A: Are we enlightened enough today?
B: There is always room for more enlightenment.
From the Aufklaerung (Enlightenment). Kant's definition: 'the courage to use your own understanding.' Past participle used as adjective. An aufgeklaerter Mensch questions everything.
having equal rights
Gleich (equal) + berechtigt (authorized).\nDie adjektivische Form von Gleichberechtigung.

A: Men and women have equal rights.
B: So it says in the law.
A: Equal partners share everything.
B: Theory and practice don't always match.
Gleich (equal) + berechtigt (authorized). The adjective form of Gleichberechtigung. Two things standing on the same legal ground.
showing solidarity
Solidar + -isch. 'Sich solidarisch erklären'\nist in Deutschland eine starke politische Aussage.

A: We must act in solidarity.
B: What does that mean concretely?
A: Standing up for each other.
B: Declaring solidarity is the first step.
Solidar + -isch. 'Sich solidarisch erklären' (to declare solidarity) is a powerful political statement in Germany. The adjective form of Solidaritaet.
unprejudiced, unbiased
Un- (nicht) + voreingenommen (voreingenommen).\nSieben Silben gegen das Vorurteil.

A: Judges must judge without bias.
B: That is the theory.
A: Can one really be unbiased?
B: One can try.
Un- (not) + voreingenommen (prejudiced). A seven-syllable antonym of voreingenommen. The negative prefix un- reverses the prejudice. Easier said than practiced.
contemporary, up to date
Zeit (time) + gemaess (according to). Was zur\naktuellen Epoche passt. Werte, die sich entwickeln.

A: Are these values still contemporary?
B: Some yes, some no.
A: What is no longer contemporary?
B: Rigid role models for example.
Zeit (time) + gemaess (according to). According to the current time. Values that evolve as society changes. What fits the era. The opposite: unzeitgemaess.
to discriminate
Vom Lateinischen 'discriminare.' Nicht trennbar.\n'Niemand darf diskriminiert werden' (AGG).

A: Nobody may be discriminated against.
B: It says so in the law.
A: Yet discrimination happens.
B: That is why we need the law.
From Latin 'discriminare.' Non-separable -ieren verb. The AGG (anti-discrimination law) makes the prohibition of discrimination legally enforceable in Germany.
to exclude, to marginalize
Aus (out) + grenzen (to border). Trennbar:\n'Sie grenzen ihn aus.' Jemanden draussen lassen.

A: One should not exclude anyone.
B: True, but it happens often.
A: Why do people exclude others?
B: Out of fear of the unknown.
Aus (out) + grenzen (to border). Separable: 'Sie grenzen ihn aus.' Drawing a line and placing someone outside it. The opposite of einschliessen (to include).
to emancipate
Vom Lateinischen 'emancipare.' Reflexiv: 'sich emanzipieren.'\nNicht trennbar. Der Weg von Abhängigkeit zu Freiheit.

A: Women had to emancipate themselves.
B: A long struggle.
A: Can one emancipate oneself from prejudices?
B: Yes, through reflection and courage.
From Latin 'emancipare.' Reflexive when meaning self-liberation. Non-separable -ieren verb. The journey from dependence to independence.
to overcome
Über (over) + winden (to wind/struggle). Stark:\nüberwand/überwunden. Nicht trennbar.

A: We must overcome prejudices.
B: That is easier said than done.
A: Did Yilmaz overcome barriers?
B: Yes, many. And she overcame them with patience.
Über (over) + winden (to wind/struggle). Strong verb: überwinden/überwand/überwunden. Not separable. Struggling over an obstacle until you are past it.

German compound nouns need connecting elements (Fugenelemente) between their parts. The most important:

Fugen-s: Meinungsfreiheit, Identitaetskrise, Lebensqualitaet. Most common after -ung, -heit, -keit, -tion, -taet, -schaft.
Fugen-n: Chancengleichheit, Generationenkonflikt. After feminine nouns ending in -e.
Fugen-en: Frauenemanzipation, Menschenwürde. After plural or weak nouns.
Fugen-er: Kinderfreundlich, Bilderbuch. After some neuter nouns.
No Fugenelement: Weltanschauung, Schulkind. Sometimes nothing is needed.

The Fugenelement does NOT indicate genitive or plural. It is purely phonetic glue.

The Fugenelement does NOT indicate genitive or plural. It is purely phonetic glue that makes compounds easier to pronounce.

Rules of thumb:
-ung always takes Fugen-s: Meinungsfreiheit, Ordnungssystem
-heit/-keit always takes Fugen-s: Freiheitskampf, Moeglichkeitsform
-tion/-taet always takes Fugen-s: Identitaetskrise, Kommunikationsproblem
-schaft always takes Fugen-s: Gesellschaftskritik, Wissenschaftsrat

Feminine nouns ending in -e often take Fugen-n: Chancengleichheit, Sonnenschein
Weak/plural nouns take Fugen-en: Menschenwürde, Frauenemanzipation

There are exceptions, but these patterns cover 90% of cases.
The linking element is not genitive and not plural. It is purely phonetic glue.
Not genitive, not plural. Pure phonetic glue. Words ending in -ung almost always take Fugen-s.
Check the verb_table: which linking element follows words ending in -tion or -taet?
The Fugenelement after nouns ending in -taet is: s

Both sentences use structures that refer to people in a general way. One uses a formal demonstrative plus a relative clause. The other uses a universal relative opener paired with a resumptive pronoun.
The formal demonstrative for 'those who' plus a noun meaning acceptance.
Those (specific ones) who are discriminated against need societal acceptance.
Aus (out) + grenzen (to border). Drawing a line and placing someone outside it. Separable verb.
Night walk through Berlin. Verumius reflects on identity, culture, and belonging. All three grammar constructs in complex combinations.
Practice all three grammar constructs in complex combinations
Master advanced identity and culture vocabulary
Build coherent argumentative texts
Berlin at night. Verumius walks home alone. He passes the corner shop, the U-Bahn entrance, the doner stand from Unit 2. All familiar now. He is processing the dinner conversation.
Everything I have experienced here has changed my identity. Whoever lives between cultures belongs to both and to neither. Yilmaz knows the same feeling. Every generation faces the same question.
He sees the Brandenburg Gate in the distance. The same gate he saw on his first morning in Berlin, map upside down. Now he walks past without stopping. Berlin is home.
diversity (formal/academic)
Vom Lateinischen 'diversitas' (Verschiedenheit).\nFormeller als Vielfalt. Unternehmensbegriff.

A: Diversity is a corporate goal.
B: What does that mean concretely?
A: Variety in teams and leadership positions.
B: Diversity management is its own department.
From Latin 'diversitas' (difference). More formal than Vielfalt. 'Diversitaetsmanagement' is a corporate department in German companies. Entered German politics in the 2000s.
assimilation
Vom Lateinischen 'assimilare' (ähnlich machen).\nAssimilation loescht Unterschiede, Integration bewahrt sie.

A: Is assimilation necessary?
B: That is controversially debated.
A: What is the difference from integration?
B: Assimilation erases differences. Integration preserves them.
From Latin 'assimilare' (to make similar). Becoming similar to the dominant culture. Germans distinguish: Assimilation erases difference, Integration preserves it.
interculturality
Inter (zwischen) + Kulturalitaet. Kulturen, die sich\ngegenseitig beeinflussen. Stärker als multikulturell.

A: Interculturality goes beyond multiculturalism.
B: How?
A: It is about active exchange between cultures.
B: Not just side by side, but with each other.
Inter (between) + Kulturalitaet. The academic concept of cultures interacting and influencing each other. Stronger than multikulturell: not just coexisting but actively exchanging.
guiding culture, dominant culture
Leit (führend) + Kultur. Ein kontroverser politischer\nBegriff. Spaltet links und rechts seit 2000.

A: What is Leitkultur?
B: A controversial idea.
A: Should there be a guiding culture?
B: Politicians have been arguing about that for decades.
Leit (guiding) + Kultur. A controversial German political term. Does Germany have a Leitkultur that immigrants should adopt? The debate divides left and right since 2000.
minority protection
Minderheiten + Schutz. Fugen-en verbindet.\nDeutschland schützt vier nationale Minderheiten.

A: Minority protection is a fundamental right.
B: Which minorities are protected?
A: Danes, Frisians, Sorbs, and Sinti/Roma.
B: It is in the constitution.
Minderheiten (minorities) + Schutz (protection). The Fugen-en connects them. Germany officially protects four national minorities: Danes, Frisians, Sorbs, and Sinti/Roma.
subculture
Sub (unter) + Kultur. Berlin ist Deutschlands\nSubkultur-Hauptstadt: Punk, Techno, Street Art.

A: Berlin has a vibrant subculture.
B: Which ones for example?
A: Techno, street art, alternative scenes.
B: Kreuzberg alone has dozens.
Sub (under) + Kultur. Berlin is Germany's subculture capital: punk, techno, street art, squatter movements. Kreuzberg alone hosts dozens of distinct Subkulturen.
cultural asset, cultural heritage
Kultur + Gut (Gut/Wert). 'Immaterielles Kulturgut'\nist UNESCO-geschützt. Deutsches Brot gehört dazu.

A: German bread is cultural heritage.
B: Recognized by UNESCO?
A: Yes, intangible cultural heritage.
B: Over 3,000 types of bread!
Kultur + Gut (good/asset). 'Immaterielles Kulturgut' (intangible cultural heritage): German bread culture, carnival, Schrebergaerten. UNESCO recognizes many German Kulturgueter.
value system, moral order
Werte (Werte) + Ordnung (Ordnung). Die strukturierte\nHierarchie gesellschaftlicher Werte.

A: Every society has a value system.
B: Who determines it?
A: The constitution sets the foundation.
B: Human dignity comes first.
Werte (values) + Ordnung (order). The structured hierarchy of a society's values. The Grundgesetz establishes Germany's official Werteordnung.
future vision
Zukunft + s (Fugen-s) + Vision. Das Fugen-s\nverbindet Zukunft mit Vision.

A: Do we have a shared future vision?
B: That is the big question.
A: Politically we argue about it.
B: But we need a vision.
Zukunft (future) + s (Fugen-s) + Vision. The Fugen-s links Zukunft to Vision. 'Eine gemeinsame Zukunftsvision' is what political leaders call for.
upheaval, radical change
Um (um/herum) + Bruch (Bruch). Stärker als Wandel:\nUmbruch impliziert Bruch und Wiederaufbau.

A: Reunification was an upheaval.
B: Yes, everything changed.
A: Is there an upheaval today?
B: Digitalization is one.
Um (around/over) + Bruch (break). Stronger than Wandel: Umbruch implies breaking and rebuilding. German reunification was an Umbruch. The word carries the weight of systemic change.
mindset, political disposition
Von 'sinnen' (nachdenken). 'Gesinnungsprüfung'\nwurde in NS- und DDR-Zeiten durchgeführt.

A: Gesinnung is a political attitude.
B: May one ask about someone's Gesinnung?
A: In a democracy: no.
B: Ideological screenings are prohibited.
From 'sinnen' (to ponder). Mindset, conviction, political disposition. 'Gesinnungsprüfung' (ideological screening) was used in both Nazi and DDR times. The word carries historical weight.
role distribution
Rollen + Verteilung. Wie Rollen in der Gesellschaft\nverteilt werden: Geschlecht, Familie, Beruf.

A: The traditional role distribution is changing.
B: Slowly but steadily.
A: Who determines the role distribution?
B: Society, culture, and each individual.
Rollen (roles) + Verteilung (distribution). How roles are assigned in society: gender roles, family roles, professional roles. 'Traditionelle Rollenverteilung' is being renegotiated.
morals, morality
Vom Lateinischen 'moralis.' Doppelbedeutung: ethische\nPrinzipien UND kollektiver Geist ('die Moral sinkt').

A: What is the moral of the story?
B: That tolerance is important.
A: Moral has two meanings.
B: Yes: ethics and morale.
From Latin 'moralis' (relating to customs). Two meanings: ethical principles and collective spirit. 'Die Moral der Geschichte' (the moral of the story). Also: 'Die Moral sinkt' (morale is sinking).
externally determined
Fremd (fremd/extern) + bestimmt (bestimmt).\nGegenteil von selbstbestimmt. Kernbegriff der Freiheitsphilosophie.

A: An externally determined life is not a free life.
B: What is the opposite?
A: Self-determined.
B: Everyone should be able to live self-determined.
Fremd (foreign/external) + bestimmt (determined). The opposite of selbstbestimmt (self-determined). A key concept in German philosophy of freedom.
racist
Rasse + -istisch. Der deutsche Diskurs verwendet\ndas Wort direkt und ohne Beschoenigung.

A: Racial discrimination is prohibited.
B: Is that in the law?
A: Yes, in the AGG.
B: The General Equal Treatment Act.
Rasse (race) + -istisch. German discourse uses the word directly and without euphemism. The AGG explicitly prohibits rassistische Benachteiligung.
stereotypical
Vom Griechischen 'stereos' (fest) + 'typos' (Eindruck).\nAuch Substantiv: 'ein Stereotyp' (das, Neutrum).

A: Stereotypical ideas are dangerous.
B: Why?
A: They simplify reality.
B: And they resist change.
From Greek 'stereos' (solid) + 'typos' (impression). A fixed, solid impression that resists change. Also a noun: 'ein Stereotyp' (a stereotype). Both adjective and noun.
to tolerate
Vom Lateinischen 'tolerare' (ertragen). Passiver\nals akzeptieren: man erträgt, statt anzunehmen.

A: Should one tolerate everything?
B: No, there are limits.
A: Tolerating is more passive than accepting.
B: Right. To tolerate means to endure.
From Latin 'tolerare' (to bear/endure). Non-separable -ieren verb. More passive than akzeptieren: you endure rather than embrace. 'Unterschiede tolerieren' (to tolerate differences).
to suppress, to repress
Ver- + draengen (drücken). Nicht trennbar.\nFreuds Verdraengung ist ein Grundstein der Psychoanalyse.

A: People suppress unpleasant memories.
B: Is that healthy?
A: In the short term perhaps. Long term no.
B: Repression leads to problems.
Ver- + draengen (to push). Not separable. Pushing something away so it disappears from view. Freud's Verdraengung (repression) is a cornerstone of psychoanalysis.
'Wer... der' and 'alles, was...' are generalizing relative clauses. 'Dasselbe' is a demonstrative reference word.
Nouns ending in -heit, -keit, or -schaft all follow the same pattern. Which linking element do they take?
The Fugenelement after nouns ending in -heit/-keit/-schaft is: s
'Erstere' = the first mentioned, the first of the two terms named.
Two structures: a free relative clause for universal statements plus a demonstrative adjective expressing sameness. Neither sameness nor uniformity is what counts.
Everything that counts is not the same opinion, but the willingness to understand others.
Prove your mastery of identity and culture vocabulary. Test all three grammar constructs. Show you can build coherent arguments about society.
Test all three grammar constructs
Prove mastery of identity vocabulary
Build coherent arguments about society
Verumius walks along the Spree. The lights of Museum Island reflect in the water. He thinks of Yilmaz, of Mia, of Heinrich. Three people, three stories, the same city.
descent, lineage
Ab (von) + Stammung (Abstammung). Woher dein\nStammbaum wächst. Enthalt 'Stamm' (Stamm/Sippe).

A: Descent plays a role in many cultures.
B: In Germany too?
A: Less than before.
B: Today what you do matters more.
Ab (from) + Stammung (stemming). Where your family tree grows from. The word contains 'Stamm' (trunk/tribe): you descend from a trunk. 'Ethnische Abstammung' (ethnic descent).
individualism
Individual + -ismus. Der westliche Wert des Individuums.\nDeutschland balanciert Individualismus und Gemeinschaft.

A: Individualism is a Western value.
B: Does it conflict with community?
A: Sometimes.
B: Germany tries to combine both.
Individual + -ismus. The Western value of the individual over the collective. Germany balances Individualismus with Gemeinschaft (community). The -ismus suffix always creates masculine nouns.
competence, skill
Vom Lateinischen 'competentia.' 'Interkulturelle Kompetenz'\nist eine formale Qualifikation auf dem Arbeitsmarkt.

A: Intercultural competence is in demand.
B: What does that mean?
A: The ability to deal with different cultures.
B: It is in every job ad.
From Latin 'competentia' (meeting together). 'Interkulturelle Kompetenz' is a formal qualification. 'Sprachkompetenz' (language competence) appears on every German job ad.
a stereotype (noun)
Vom Griechischen 'stereos' + 'typos.' Neutrum.\n'Stereotype abbauen' (Stereotypen abbauen).

A: Stereotypes are simplified images.
B: Everyone has them.
A: Can you break down stereotypes?
B: Through encounter and reflection.
From Greek 'stereos' (solid) + 'typos' (impression). Neuter as a noun. The fixed impression that refuses to change. 'Stereotype abbauen' (to break down stereotypes).
someone, somebody
Je (ever) + man (one) + d. Kann dekliniert werden:\njemandes, jemandem, jemanden. Oft undekliniert.

A: Someone called.
B: Who?
A: I don't know. Someone from the university.
B: Did someone leave a message?
Je (ever) + man (one) + d. Can decline: jemandes, jemandem, jemanden. But in speech, often left undeclined. The positive counterpart to niemand.
nobody, no one
Nie (never) + mand. Die Verneinung von jemand.\nKann wie jemand dekliniert werden.

A: Nobody is perfect.
B: That is true.
A: Nobody should be excluded.
B: On that we agree.
Nie (never) + mand. The negative of jemand. 'Niemand ist perfekt' (Nobody is perfect). Can decline like jemand but often left undeclined in speech.
someone or other, anyone at all
Irgend (irgend) + wer (wer). Unbestimmter als jemand.\nDas irgend- Praefix fuegt Unbestimmtheit hinzu.

A: Someone or other has to do it.
B: But who?
A: Someone or other will step forward.
B: Hopefully soon.
Irgend (some/any) + wer (who). More vague than jemand. The irgend- prefix adds indefiniteness. 'Irgendwer muss es tun' (Someone or other has to do it).
something or other, anything at all
Irgend + etwas. Unbestimmter als etwas allein.\nUmgangssprachlich verkürzt zu 'irgendwas.'

A: Something or other is not right.
B: What do you mean?
A: I don't know exactly. Something feels wrong.
B: Maybe the atmosphere.
Irgend + etwas (something). Vaguer than etwas alone. Colloquially shortened to 'irgendwas.' 'Irgendetwas stimmt nicht' (Something or other is not right).
all, every single one
Von 'samt' (zusammen mit). Stärker als 'alle':\nbetont Vollständigkeit. Keine Ausnahmen.

A: Every single prejudice must be broken down.
B: All of them?
A: Yes, every single one. Without exception.
B: That is a high goal.
From 'samt' (together with). Stronger than 'alle': it emphasizes completeness. No exceptions. 'Saemtliche Vorurteile abbauen' (to break down every single prejudice).
every possible, any whatsoever
Sehr formell. Stärker als jeder.\nLässt null Raum für Ausnahmen. Juristisch und literarisch.

A: Any discrimination whatsoever is prohibited.
B: Even indirect?
A: Yes, every possible form.
B: The law leaves no room.
Very formal. Stronger than jeder. Leaves zero room for exceptions. 'Jegliche Diskriminierung ist verboten' (Any discrimination whatsoever is prohibited). Legal and literary register.
every single, any whatsoever (literary)
Jed + weder (einer von beiden). Noch formeller\nals jeglicher. Literarisch und juristisch. Selten gesprochen.

A: Every single form of racism is unacceptable.
B: That is a strong statement.
A: Jedweder means: without any exception.
B: A word for official texts.
Jed + weder (either). Even more formal than jeglicher. Literary and legal register. Rarely spoken. 'Jedwede Form von Rassismus' (every single form of racism).
each and every one
Ein + jeder. Gehobener Stil. Emphatischer als\neinfaches 'jeder.' Fast biblisch im Ton.

A: Each and every one bears responsibility.
B: For what?
A: For society.
B: Each and every one can contribute something.
Ein + jeder. Elevated register. More emphatic than simple 'jeder.' Almost biblical in tone. 'Ein jeder trägt Verantwortung' (Each and every one bears responsibility).
necessarily
Notwendig (notwendig) + -erweise. Ein akademischer\nQualifizierer. Präzisionssprache.

A: Diversity does not necessarily lead to tolerance.
B: Why not?
A: You have to actively work on it.
B: Diversity alone is not enough.
Notwendig (necessary) + -erweise. An academic qualifier. Precision language. 'Vielfalt führt nicht notwendigerweise zu Toleranz' (Diversity does not necessarily lead to tolerance).
inevitably, necessarily
Zwang (Zwang) + laeufig (laufend). Stärker als\nnotwendigerweise. Etwas, das nicht aufzuhalten ist.

A: That inevitably leads to conflicts.
B: Always?
A: Not always, but often.
B: Inevitably sounds final.
Zwang (compulsion) + laeufig (running). Running by compulsion: something that cannot be stopped. Stronger than notwendigerweise. 'Das führt zwangslaeufig zu Konflikten.'
to cope, to manage
Zurecht (richtig) + kommen. Trennbar:\n'Ich komme damit zurecht.' Sich zurechtfinden.

A: Are you coping with the situation?
B: It is not easy.
A: One has to manage.
B: Yes, step by step.
Zurecht (rightly/properly) + kommen (to come). Separable: 'Ich komme damit zurecht.' Finding your way properly. 'Mit der neuen Situation zurechtkommen' (to cope with the new situation).
to appreciate, to value
Wert (Wert) + schätzen (schätzen). Aktiver als\nrespektieren: man bewertet und ehrt aktiv.

A: We should appreciate diversity.
B: What does appreciate mean exactly?
A: To recognize and honor the value.
B: More than just respecting.
Wert (value) + schätzen (to estimate). Estimating someone's worth highly. More active than respektieren: you actively assess and honor value. 'Vielfalt wertschätzen' (to appreciate diversity).
'Wer...der' is a generalizing relative clause. 'Derjenige, der...' is a demonstrative with relative clause.
The first of the two terms mentioned. 'Toleranz' was named first.
Tolerance and respect: the former is passive, the latter active.
The base noun belongs to a suffix group that consistently takes one specific linking element. Check the verb_table for which suffixes always use the same connector.

Three constructs: a generalizing relative clause opener (das/alles + was), a demonstrative for sameness (ending in -selbe), and the correct Fugenelement for 'Meinung'. Three different grammatical layers in one sentence.
That which society needs is not the same opinion, but diversity of freedom of opinion.
Both are stronger than 'jede.' They leave zero room for exceptions. Legal and literary register.
Feminine nouns ending in -heit follow a different pattern than -ung nouns. Look at compounds like Menschenwürde or Staatenbund for the pattern.
The Fugenelement after nouns like Minderheit is: en
Mia fights for equal rights. Yilmaz mediates between cultures. KD trusts paper. Heinrich preserves the language. And me? I collect their stories.

Verumius's apartment, late at night. The dinner conversation echoes in his mind. He opens his notebook and writes: 'What I have learned: Identity is not a place. Identity is a movement.'
Whoever lives between worlds does not have to choose. Those who are both see both more clearly. That is the insight of this evening.
He closes the notebook. Tomorrow he is thinking about going home. What will it feel like to return? The question of Heimat is approaching.
insight, realization
Er- + Kenntnis (Wissen). Wissen, das durch Vernunft\ngewonnen wird. Kants zentraler Begriff.

A: That was an important insight.
B: Which one?
A: That identity is a movement.
B: Arriving at an insight takes time.
Er- + Kenntnis (knowledge). Knowledge gained through reason. Kant's central concept. 'Zu einer Erkenntnis kommen' (to arrive at an insight). The philosophical word for real understanding.
concept, term
Von 'begreifen' (greifen/verstehen). Etwas, das man\nmit dem Verstand gegriffen hat.

A: What is the term for this concept?
B: Identity.
A: Defining the term is difficult.
B: Yes, some concepts elude definition.
From 'begreifen' (to grasp). Literally: something you have grasped with your mind. 'Den Begriff definieren' (to define the term). Begreifen = to grasp physically and intellectually.
to assimilate
Vom Lateinischen 'assimilare.' Reflexiv: 'sich assimilieren.'\nAssimilation loescht Unterschiede.

A: Must one assimilate?
B: That is disputed.
A: To assimilate means to give up differences.
B: Integration preserves them.
From Latin 'assimilare.' Reflexive when meaning self-assimilation. The debate: integration preserves identity, assimilation dissolves it. 'Sich an die Kultur assimilieren.'
to derive, to deduce
Ab (von) + leiten (führen). Trennbar:\n'Ich leite daraus ab.' Akademisches Vokabular.

A: What can one derive from that?
B: That tolerance must be active.
A: I derive a different insight.
B: Which one?
Ab (from) + leiten (to lead). Separable: 'Ich leite daraus ab.' Leading a conclusion away from evidence. Academic reasoning vocabulary.
to influence
Be- + Einfluss (Einfluss). Nicht trennbar.\nDas be- Praefix macht Einfluss zum Verb.

A: Heritage influences identity.
B: How strongly?
A: Strongly, but not completely.
B: One can also break free from it.
Be- + Einfluss (influence). Not separable. The be- prefix makes Einfluss (noun) into a verb. Making the influence flow into something. 'Die Herkunft beeinflusst die Identitaet.'
to define
Vom Lateinischen 'definire' (Grenzen setzen).\nNicht trennbar. Grenzen um ein Konzept ziehen.

A: How does one define identity?
B: That is difficult.
A: Everyone defines it differently.
B: That is exactly what makes the concept so complex.
From Latin 'definire' (to set boundaries). Non-separable -ieren verb. Drawing boundaries around a concept. Same word across European languages.
to classify, to categorize
Ein (in) + ordnen (ordnen). Trennbar: 'Ich ordne das ein.'\nKann negativ sein: Menschen widersetzen sich dem Eingeordnetwerden.

A: One should not hastily classify people.
B: Why not?
A: Because categories simplify.
B: Every person is more complex than any category.
Ein (in) + ordnen (to order). Separable: 'Ich ordne das ein.' Placing something into an order. Can be negative: people resist being eingeordnet (classified).
to put on equal footing
Gleich (gleich) + stellen (stellen). Trennbar.\nDie juristische Version von Gleichheit.

A: All people should be put on equal footing.
B: In the law they are.
A: They are formally made equal.
B: But reality lags behind.
Gleich (equal) + stellen (to place). Separable: 'Man stellt sie gleich.' The legal version of equality: placing two things at the same level.
to sensitize, to raise awareness
Sensibel + -isieren. Jemanden für ein Thema\nempfindlicher machen. Bildungs- und Politikvokabular.

A: We must raise awareness of discrimination.
B: How?
A: Through workshops and education.
B: Sensitization is the first step.
Sensibel (sensitive) + -isieren. Making someone more sensitive to an issue. Educational and political vocabulary. 'Für Diskriminierung sensibilisieren' (to raise awareness of discrimination).
to rethink, to change one's thinking
Um (um/herum) + denken. Trennbar: 'Wir denken um.'\nEin Paradigmenwechsel in einem Wort.

A: Society must rethink.
B: In what regard?
A: On the topic of identity.
B: Rethinking takes time and courage.
Um (re-/around) + denken (to think). Separable: 'Wir denken um.' Turning your thinking around. A paradigm shift in one word. 'Die Gesellschaft muss umdenken.'
future-oriented
Zukunft + s (Fugen-s) + orientiert. Dreiteiliges\nKompositum mit Fugen-s. Nach vorne schauen.

A: A future-oriented society invests in education.
B: And in technology.
A: Thinking in a future-oriented way is important.
B: The future begins today.
Zukunft (future) + s (Fugen-s) + orientiert (oriented). Three-part compound with Fugen-s. Looking forward rather than backward. 'Eine zukunftsorientierte Gesellschaft.'
to identify (pinpoint)
Vom Lateinischen 'identificare.' Nicht trennbar.\nOhne 'sich': erkennen. Mit 'sich': sich identifizieren mit.

A: Identifying problems is the first step.
B: And then?
A: Finding solutions.
B: One must precisely identify what the problem is.
From Latin 'identificare.' Non-separable -ieren verb. Without 'sich': to pinpoint or recognize. With 'sich': to identify with. 'Probleme identifizieren' (to identify problems).
one, you (impersonal), people
[V1-Auffrischung auf B2-Niveau.] Der unpersönliche\nPronomen. Nicht gross (nicht Mann). Häufiger als im Englischen.

A: People say that Berlin is cosmopolitan.
B: Is that true?
A: One experiences it daily.
B: One gets used to it.
The impersonal pronoun. Not capitalized (unlike Mann = man). 'Man' is used far more in German than 'one' in English. 'Man sagt' (people say) is everywhere.
some, many a
[V1-Auffrischung.] Dekliniert wie dieser.\n'Manche' (Plural) = einige. Weniger bestimmt als 'viele.'

A: Many a person searches a lifetime for identity.
B: Some never find it.
A: Some early, some late.
B: That is human.
Some, many a. Declines like dieser. 'Manche' (plural) = some. A less definite quantifier than 'einige' or 'viele.' 'Mancher Mensch' (many a person).

German has unique words for identity concepts that other languages can barely translate.

Weltanschauung: your comprehensive view of the world.
Bildung: not just education, but self-formation.
Heimat: the place where you belong (not just birthplace).
Zugehörigkeit: the feeling of being part of something.
Sehnsucht: deep longing for something unreachable.

These words shape how Germans think about identity. Language does not just describe the concepts. It creates them.
Whoever speaks German thinks differently about identity.

Germany produced some of the world's most influential identity philosophers. Kant explored Erkenntnis (knowledge through reason). Hegel wrote about Bewusstsein (consciousness) and its development. Marx examined Entfremdung (alienation). Heidegger questioned Dasein (being-there). Arendt analyzed Zugehörigkeit (belonging) in political life.\n\nThese thinkers did not just use German words. They created concepts that could only exist in German. Weltanschauung, Bildung, Heimat, Sehnsucht: these are untranslatable because they carry centuries of philosophical weight. Learning German means learning to think in these categories.
Count the distinct constructs: a free relative clause opener, a linking element inside a compound noun, and a demonstrative pronoun referring back. All three are present in this single sentence.
After indefinite pronouns like 'alles' or 'nichts', the relative pronoun is not the usual definite article form. It is a special relative word.
Everything that I have experienced in Germany has shaped me.
Nouns ending in -heit, -keit, and -schaft all follow the same rule. Look at the suffix family pattern in the verb_table.
Generalizing relative clause (wer...der) + demonstrative (diese). 'Wer andere respektiert, der wird selbst respektiert.'
Whoever respects others is respected. This insight is timeless.
Think about what happens to your original identity in each process. One preserves differences, the other dissolves them.
